verses.text: In the twelfth year of Ahaz king of Judah began Hoshea the son of Elah to reign in Samaria over Israel nine years. And he did that which was evil in the sight of the LORD, but not as the kings of Israel that were before him. Guidance: The last king of Israel. Hoshea son of Elah — who had seized the throne by assassinating Pekah (15:30) — reigns nine years as the final king of the northern kingdom. His verdict is curious: 'he did that which was evil in the sight of the LORD, but not as the kings of Israel that were before him.' He was evil, yet somehow less so than his predecessors. (Jewish tradition suggests he removed the border-guards that had prevented northerners from going to Jerusalem to worship, or relaxed some of the calf-cult enforcement.) The chapter is showing us a sobering irony at the kingdom's end: the LAST king was, in some measure, the LEAST wicked of the northern line — and yet it is on his watch that the kingdom falls. The chapter is teaching us a hard truth about national judgment: the accumulated guilt of generations is not always discharged on the worst individual but falls when the appointed time arrives. Hoshea was no Jeroboam or Ahab, but the LORD's patience, extended across nineteen kings and two centuries of calf-worship, had finally run out. The chapter is teaching us that a slightly-improved leader cannot reverse the momentum of a nation's long rebellion at the eleventh hour — the harvest of two hundred years comes due, and it comes due now. The relative mildness of the last king only deepens the tragedy: the kingdom does not fall because Hoshea was uniquely evil, but because Israel as a whole had finally exhausted the time for repentance.

Against him came up Shalmaneser king of Assyria; and Hoshea became his servant, and gave him presents. And the king of Assyria found conspiracy in Hoshea: for he had sent messengers to So king of Egypt, and brought no present to the king of Assyria, as he had done year by year: therefore the king of Assyria shut him up, and bound him in prison. Then the king of Assyria came up throughout all the land, and went up to Samaria, and besieged it three years. In the ninth year of Hoshea the king of Assyria took Samaria, and carried Israel away into Assyria, and placed them in Halah and in Habor by the river of Gozan, and in the cities of the Medes. Guidance: The fall of Samaria. The end comes in stages. Shalmaneser V of Assyria invades; Hoshea submits and pays annual tribute. But Hoshea gambles on rebellion — he secretly sends to 'So king of Egypt' for help and withholds the tribute. The gamble fails: Egypt does not save him (Egypt never did save those who leaned on it — Isa 30:1-3), Assyria discovers the conspiracy, imprisons Hoshea, and besieges Samaria. The capital holds out for three years — a testament to its fortifications — but in Hoshea's ninth year 'the king of Assyria took Samaria, and carried Israel away into Assyria.' The northern kingdom, founded by Jeroboam I two centuries earlier, ceases to exist. The Israelites are deported to Halah, to Habor by the river Gozan, and to the cities of the Medes — scattered across the Assyrian empire, the policy designed to erase national identity. (These exiles become the 'ten lost tribes,' absorbed and never to return as a distinct people.) The chapter is showing us the catastrophe the prophets had warned of for generations finally arriving. Hoshea's last-ditch maneuver captures the kingdom's whole foolish strategy in miniature: trusting Egypt against Assyria, playing the powers off each other, anything but trusting the LORD. The chapter is teaching us that the habit of leaning on foreign powers instead of God — begun when Israel split from David's house and built its own gods — ends in being swallowed by a foreign power. The chapter is also teaching us the finality of judgment long delayed: for two hundred years the LORD sent prophets, oppressions, and mercies; now the door closes. Samaria falls; Israel is carried away; and the next verses will explain, with unflinching theological clarity, exactly why.

For so it was, that the children of Israel had sinned against the LORD their God, which had brought them up out of the land of Egypt, from under the hand of Pharaoh king of Egypt, and had feared other gods, And walked in the statutes of the heathen, whom the LORD cast out from before the children of Israel, and of the kings of Israel, which they had made. And the children of Israel did secretly those things that were not right against the LORD their God, and they built them high places in all their cities, from the tower of the watchmen to the fenced city. And they set them up images and groves in every high hill, and under every green tree: And there they burnt incense in all the high places, as did the heathen whom the LORD carried away before them; and wrought wicked things to provoke the LORD to anger: For they served idols, whereof the LORD had said unto them, Ye shall not do this thing. Guidance: The great explanation begins — WHY Israel fell. Having reported the fall, the narrator now pauses for the longest theological reflection in the books of Kings — an inspired sermon explaining exactly why the kingdom was destroyed. It opens with the root: 'the children of Israel had sinned against the LORD their God, which had brought them up out of the land of Egypt.' The indictment is framed by the redemption — they sinned against the very God who had rescued them from Pharaoh. Their core sin was that they 'feared other gods' and 'walked in the statutes of the heathen' whom the LORD had driven out before them — they imitated the very nations whose abominations had cost those nations the land. The catalog of sin follows: secret evils, high places in every city (from the smallest watchtower-hamlet to the largest fortified city — i.e., everywhere), images and Asherah-groves on every hill and under every green tree, incense at all the high places 'as did the heathen,' and the service of idols the LORD had expressly forbidden. The chapter is showing us that the exile was not a political accident or mere Assyrian aggression — it was the LORD's just judgment on persistent, comprehensive idolatry. The narrator wants no reader to conclude that Assyria simply happened to be stronger; the deepest cause was Israel's sin against her redeeming God. The chapter is teaching us the gravity of forsaking the God who saved you. The exodus-deliverance was the foundation of the whole covenant relationship; to fear other gods after such a rescue was the basest ingratitude and the deepest betrayal. The chapter is also teaching us how thoroughly idolatry had penetrated — not in a few shrines but 'in all their cities,' 'every high hill,' 'every green tree.' This was not the sin of a few kings but the saturation of the whole society. The chapter is teaching us that when a people make idolatry their universal habit, against the express word of the God who redeemed them, judgment is not cruelty but justice long withheld.

Yet the LORD testified against Israel, and against Judah, by all the prophets, and by all the seers, saying, Turn ye from your evil ways, and keep my commandments and my statutes, according to all the law which I commanded your fathers, and which I sent to you by my servants the prophets. Notwithstanding they would not hear, but hardened their necks, like to the neck of their fathers, that did not believe in the LORD their God. And they rejected his statutes, and his covenant that he made with their fathers, and his testimonies which he testified against them; and they followed vanity, and became vain, and went after the heathen that were round about them, concerning whom the LORD had charged them, that they should not do like them. And they left all the commandments of the LORD their God, and made them molten images, even two calves, and made a grove, and worshipped all the host of heaven, and served Baal. And they caused their sons and their daughters to pass through the fire, and used divination and enchantments, and sold themselves to do evil in the sight of the LORD, to provoke him to anger. Guidance: The LORD's long warning and Israel's hardened refusal. The sermon now stresses that the judgment came only after centuries of warning. 'Yet the LORD testified against Israel, and against Judah, by all the prophets, and by all the seers, saying, Turn ye from your evil ways.' For two hundred years the LORD sent prophet after prophet — Elijah, Elisha, Amos, Hosea, and many unnamed — all with the same merciful summons: turn, repent, keep My commandments. The tragedy is in the response: 'Notwithstanding they would not hear, but hardened their necks, like to the neck of their fathers, that did not believe in the LORD their God.' The root sin is named precisely — unbelief ('did not believe in the LORD their God'). They 'rejected his statutes, and his covenant... and his testimonies'; they 'followed vanity, and became vain' (the haunting phrase: those who pursue empty idols become empty themselves — we are shaped into the likeness of what we worship). The catalog of their sins is total: the two calves, the Asherah, the worship of the host of heaven (astral cults), the service of Baal, child sacrifice ('caused their sons and their daughters to pass through the fire'), divination and sorcery, and finally 'sold themselves to do evil' — a chilling image of willing, total surrender to wickedness. The chapter is showing us that the LORD's judgment was preceded by extraordinary patience and persistent warning. He did not strike without notice; He sent His servants the prophets again and again, pleading for repentance. The chapter is teaching us the central diagnosis of Israel's ruin: 'they would not hear.' The fatal disease was not ignorance but a hardened, unbelieving refusal to listen to God's clear, repeated word. The chapter is also teaching us the profound truth that 'they followed vanity, and became vain' — idolatry does not merely offend God; it hollows out the worshipper, deforming people into the emptiness of the lifeless things they serve. And the warning extends to Judah ('against Israel, AND against Judah') — the south is being put on notice that the same refusal will bring the same end. The chapter is teaching us that the most dangerous condition of all is a heart that has heard God's warnings so often it has stopped listening — a stiff neck that mistakes its own hardness for stability while judgment gathers.

Therefore the LORD was very angry with Israel, and removed them out of his sight: there was none left but the tribe of Judah only. Also Judah kept not the commandments of the LORD their God, but walked in the statutes of Israel which they made. And the LORD rejected all the seed of Israel, and afflicted them, and delivered them into the hand of spoilers, until he had cast them out of his sight. Guidance: The verdict — removed from His sight, and a warning to Judah. The sermon reaches its verdict: 'Therefore the LORD was very angry with Israel, and removed them out of his sight: there was none left but the tribe of Judah only.' The northern kingdom is gone; only Judah (with Benjamin and the faithful absorbed into it) remains of the twelve tribes. But the narrator immediately refuses to let Judah feel safe: 'Also Judah kept not the commandments of the LORD their God, but walked in the statutes of Israel which they made.' Judah is on the same road — imitating the north's sins (as Ahaz had just demonstrated, ch 16). The verdict is restated with terrible weight: 'the LORD rejected all the seed of Israel, and afflicted them, and delivered them into the hand of spoilers, until he had cast them out of his sight.' The chapter is showing us the awful reality of being 'removed out of His sight' / 'cast out of his sight' — the covenant blessing was the LORD's presence with His people; the ultimate judgment is the withdrawal of that presence, expulsion from the land where He had set His name. The chapter is teaching us that the deepest loss in judgment is not the land or the kingship but the nearness of God — to be cast out of His sight is the inverse of the covenant promise 'I will be your God and you shall be my people.' The chapter is also teaching us, with the pointed note about Judah, that the fall of the north is not merely the north's story — it is a mirror and a warning held up to the south. Judah watched Israel sin and saw Israel fall, yet 'walked in the statutes of Israel which they made.' The chapter is teaching us the danger of failing to learn from another's judgment: Judah had a front-row seat to the consequences of apostasy and still followed the same path, which is why the books of Kings will end, eventually, with Judah's own exile to Babylon. To see judgment fall on others and not repent is to invite the same judgment on oneself.

For he rent Israel from the house of David; and they made Jeroboam the son of Nebat king: and Jeroboam drave Israel from following the LORD, and made them sin a great sin. For the children of Israel walked in all the sins of Jeroboam which he did; they departed not from them; Until the LORD removed Israel out of his sight, as he had said by all his servants the prophets. So was Israel carried away out of their own land to Assyria unto this day. Guidance: Tracing the ruin to its source — Jeroboam's great sin. The sermon traces the catastrophe all the way back to its origin: 'he rent Israel from the house of David; and they made Jeroboam the son of Nebat king: and Jeroboam drave Israel from following the LORD, and made them sin a great sin.' The northern kingdom's whole existence began in the schism from David's house (1 Kgs 12), and its founding king, Jeroboam I, set up the golden calves to keep his people from going up to Jerusalem to worship — 'making them sin a great sin.' Every subsequent king 'walked in all the sins of Jeroboam... they departed not from them' (the refrain that has echoed through nineteen kings). The result: 'Until the LORD removed Israel out of his sight, as he had said by all his servants the prophets. So was Israel carried away out of their own land to Assyria unto this day.' The chapter is showing us the long arc of consequence from a single foundational sin. Jeroboam's calves were not a minor compromise but the original poison that flowed through the entire two-century life of the kingdom — every king drank from it, none turned from it, and it ended in exile exactly as the prophets had foretold (Ahijah had prophesied it to Jeroboam himself, 1 Kgs 14:15-16). The chapter is teaching us that sins built into the foundations of an institution or a life have a terrible staying power — Jeroboam's choice to secure his political position by counterfeit worship became the inescapable groove in which the whole nation ran to its destruction. The chapter is also teaching us, with the repeated note 'as he had said by all his servants the prophets,' the perfect reliability of God's word: what the prophets foretold across two centuries came to pass to the letter. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's warnings are not idle threats; the exile that seemed unthinkable when Jeroboam first set up his calves was, in the end, the precise outworking of the word God had spoken — and the phrase 'unto this day' reminds the reader that the loss was permanent: the ten tribes never came home.

And the king of Assyria brought men from Babylon, and from Cuthah, and from Ava, and from Hamath, and from Sepharvaim, and placed them in the cities of Samaria instead of the children of Israel: and they possessed Samaria, and dwelt in the cities thereof. And so it was at the beginning of their dwelling there, that they feared not the LORD: therefore the LORD sent lions among them, which slew some of them. Wherefore they spake to the king of Assyria, saying, The nations which thou hast removed, and placed in the cities of Samaria, know not the manner of the God of the land: therefore he hath sent lions among them, and, behold, they slay them, because they know not the manner of the God of the land. Then the king of Assyria commanded, saying, Carry thither one of the priests whom ye brought from thence; and let them go and dwell there, and let him teach them the manner of the God of the land. Then one of the priests whom they had carried away from Samaria came and dwelt in Bethel, and taught them how they should fear the LORD. Guidance: The resettlement of Samaria and the lions. Assyrian policy did not leave conquered lands empty; it transplanted other conquered peoples into them. So the king of Assyria brings men from Babylon, Cuthah, Ava, Hamath, and Sepharvaim and settles them in the emptied cities of Samaria. These pagan newcomers 'feared not the LORD' — they did not worship the God of the land they now occupied. The LORD's response is striking: 'therefore the LORD sent lions among them, which slew some of them.' The settlers interpret this (correctly, in part) as the anger of 'the God of the land' whose 'manner' they did not know. They appeal to the Assyrian king, who pragmatically orders that one of the exiled Israelite priests be sent back to teach them 'the manner of the God of the land.' A priest comes and settles in Bethel (ironically, the old calf-shrine city) and teaches them 'how they should fear the LORD.' The chapter is showing us that the LORD's authority extends over the very land even after His people are removed from it — the pagan settlers cannot simply ignore Him; He asserts His claim through the lions. The chapter is teaching us a profound truth: the God of Israel is not a local deity who departs when His people are deported. He remains the LORD of the land and of all peoples, and even pagans transplanted into His territory must reckon with Him. The chapter is also setting up the deeply ironic and tragic origin of the Samaritans (the mixed population that results). The priest sent to teach them was almost certainly a priest of the calf-cult from Bethel — so the 'fear of the LORD' these settlers learn is the corrupted, syncretistic northern version, not the true worship of Jerusalem. The chapter is teaching us that half-knowledge of God, taught by a compromised teacher, produces a compromised religion — which the next verses will lay bare as the settlers add the LORD to their existing idols rather than abandoning the idols for the LORD.

Then the king of Assyria commanded, saying, Carry thither one of the priests whom ye brought from thence; and let them go and dwell there, and let him teach them the manner of the God of the land. Then one of the priests whom they had carried away from Samaria came and dwelt in Bethel, and taught them how they should fear the LORD. Guidance: The resettlement of Samaria and the lions. Assyrian policy did not leave conquered lands empty; it transplanted other conquered peoples into them. So the king of Assyria brings men from Babylon, Cuthah, Ava, Hamath, and Sepharvaim and settles them in the emptied cities of Samaria. These pagan newcomers 'feared not the LORD' — they did not worship the God of the land they now occupied. The LORD's response is striking: 'therefore the LORD sent lions among them, which slew some of them.' The settlers interpret this (correctly, in part) as the anger of 'the God of the land' whose 'manner' they did not know. They appeal to the Assyrian king, who pragmatically orders that one of the exiled Israelite priests be sent back to teach them 'the manner of the God of the land.' A priest comes and settles in Bethel (ironically, the old calf-shrine city) and teaches them 'how they should fear the LORD.' The chapter is showing us that the LORD's authority extends over the very land even after His people are removed from it — the pagan settlers cannot simply ignore Him; He asserts His claim through the lions. The chapter is teaching us a profound truth: the God of Israel is not a local deity who departs when His people are deported. He remains the LORD of the land and of all peoples, and even pagans transplanted into His territory must reckon with Him. The chapter is also setting up the deeply ironic and tragic origin of the Samaritans (the mixed population that results). The priest sent to teach them was almost certainly a priest of the calf-cult from Bethel — so the 'fear of the LORD' these settlers learn is the corrupted, syncretistic northern version, not the true worship of Jerusalem. The chapter is teaching us that half-knowledge of God, taught by a compromised teacher, produces a compromised religion — which the next verses will lay bare as the settlers add the LORD to their existing idols rather than abandoning the idols for the LORD.

Howbeit every nation made gods of their own, and put them in the houses of the high places which the Samaritans had made, every nation in their cities wherein they dwelt. And the men of Babylon made Succothbenoth, and the men of Cuth made Nergal, and the men of Hamath made Ashima, And the Avites made Nibhaz and Tartak, and the Sepharvites burnt their children in fire to Adrammelech and Anammelech, the gods of Sepharvaim. So they feared the LORD, and made unto themselves of the lowest of them priests of the high places, which sacrificed for them in the houses of the high places. They feared the LORD, and served their own gods, after the manner of the nations whom they carried away from thence. Guidance: Syncretism — fearing the LORD and serving idols. The teaching had no transforming effect. Each transplanted nation kept making its own gods and installing them in the high-place shrines the former Israelites had built. The narrator catalogs the imported idols: Succoth-benoth (Babylon), Nergal (Cuth), Ashima (Hamath), Nibhaz and Tartak (Avites), and the child-burning gods Adrammelech and Anammelech (Sepharvaim) — including, horrifically, child sacrifice continuing among the new population. And then the chapter's defining phrase, repeated twice: 'They feared the LORD, and served their own gods.' They added the LORD to their pantheon — acknowledging Him as the local deity to avoid the lions, while keeping all their own idols and appointing 'the lowest of them' as priests of the high places. The chapter is showing us the essence of syncretism: not the rejection of the LORD, but the refusal to worship Him ALONE. These settlers did not deny the LORD; they simply made Him one god among many, fitting Him into their existing idolatrous framework. The chapter is teaching us that 'fearing the LORD and serving other gods' is not a halfway-acceptable middle position — it is itself a form of unfaithfulness, because the LORD demands exclusive worship ('Thou shalt have no other gods before me'). To acknowledge the true God while keeping idols is to fail the first commandment as surely as outright atheism does. The chapter is teaching us the subtle and enduring danger of divided worship: it feels like piety (they did 'fear the LORD'), it feels like prudence (covering all the spiritual bases), but it is in fact the very sin for which Israel was exiled — and the new inhabitants reproduce it from the start. The mixed religion of these Samaritan settlers becomes a lasting object lesson in the Bible's insistence that the LORD will not share His glory with another.

Unto this day they do after the former manners: they fear not the LORD, neither do they after their statutes, or after their ordinances, or after the law and commandment which the LORD commanded the children of Jacob, whom he named Israel; With whom the LORD had made a covenant, and charged them, saying, Ye shall not fear other gods, nor bow yourselves to them, nor serve them, nor sacrifice to them: But the LORD, who brought you up out of the land of Egypt with great power and a stretched out arm, him shall ye fear, and him shall ye worship, and to him shall ye do sacrifice. And the statutes, and the ordinances, and the law, and the commandment, which he wrote for you, ye shall observe to do for evermore; and ye shall not fear other gods. And the covenant that I have made with you ye shall not forget; neither shall ye fear other gods. But the LORD your God ye shall fear; and he shall deliver you out of the hand of all your enemies. Howbeit they did not hearken, but they did after their former manner. So these nations feared the LORD, and served their graven images, both their children, and their children's children: as did their fathers, so do they unto this day. Guidance: The closing verdict — and the covenant's clear demand. The narrator delivers his final assessment of the Samaritan population, and through them, restates the covenant's central demand. 'Unto this day they do after the former manners: they fear not the LORD' — despite their token acknowledgment of Him, their divided worship means they do not truly fear Him at all. The narrator then quotes the heart of the covenant the LORD made with 'the children of Jacob, whom he named Israel': 'Ye shall not fear other gods, nor bow yourselves to them... But the LORD, who brought you up out of the land of Egypt with great power and a stretched out arm, him shall ye fear, and him shall ye worship, and to him shall ye do sacrifice.' The demand is exclusive and absolute — fear the LORD ALONE, the God of the exodus, and 'fear other gods' no more. And the covenant carries a promise: 'he shall deliver you out of the hand of all your enemies.' But 'they did not hearken, but they did after their former manner' — and the chapter ends with the haunting summary that captures the entire tragedy of the chapter and the kingdom: 'these nations feared the LORD, and served their graven images... as did their fathers, so do they unto this day.' The chapter is closing the death of the northern kingdom by exposing the false religion that replaced it — and in doing so, restating the one thing the LORD required all along: undivided worship. The chapter is teaching us, definitively, that 'feared the LORD and served their graven images' is the epitaph of failure, not a workable compromise. The whole disaster of the northern kingdom — and now of its replacement population — comes down to this single refusal to worship the LORD alone. The chapter is teaching us that the covenant offers a stunning exchange: exclusive devotion to the God of the exodus in return for His deliverance from all enemies. Israel refused the exchange and lost everything. The chapter is also drawing the line that will define the rest of redemptive history: the LORD will not be ONE god among many; He must be God alone, or He is not truly feared at all. As the chapter has shown across forty-one verses, the deepest cause of every ruin — the kingdom's and the settlers' alike — is the divided heart that tries to keep both the LORD and its idols. The chapter is teaching us, as a warning aimed squarely at every reader (and at Judah next), that the only acceptable worship is wholehearted, exclusive devotion to the LORD who redeemed His people 'with great power and a stretched out arm.'
